$comment: Dimension reference for front-end and agent consumption. Extracted from CLAUDE.md, docs/audit-dimensions-v2.0.md, and config/scoring-weights.json. 56 dimensions across 7 clusters. Score levels describe the 0-4 scale per dimension. Tier 2 dimensions (4.16-4.27) use 0/1/2 scale.

0.1_platform_architecture_clarity.name: Platform Architecture Clarity
0.1_platform_architecture_clarity.description: Does the system have a coherent, documented strategy for serving its target platforms, and is that strategy legible to an AI agent?
0.1_platform_architecture_clarity.plain_description: Whether the system has a clear, documented strategy for which platforms it serves, written in a way an AI tool can understand and act on.

score_levels.0: No platform strategy documented or inferable. Multiple conflicting conventions.
score_levels.1: Platform target inferable but undocumented. Major gaps in platform-specific handling.
score_levels.2: Platform strategy implicit. An agent can infer the target but must rely on convention rather than declaration.
score_levels.3: Platform strategy documented with minor gaps. Most platform-specific decisions are explicit.
score_levels.4: Coherent, documented platform strategy. Single source of truth for tokens and components. Platform differences explicit and principled.

1.1_token_implementation.name: Token Implementation
1.1_token_implementation.description: Are design tokens implemented as Figma Variables? Are hardcoded values present where tokens should be used?
1.1_token_implementation.plain_description: Whether colours, spacing, and other design values are defined as named Figma Variables rather than typed in as individual numbers.

score_levels.0: No Figma Variables. All values hardcoded.
score_levels.1: Variables exist for one category only (e.g. colour). Most values hardcoded.
score_levels.2: Variables for 2-3 categories but significant hardcoded values remain.
score_levels.3: Variables for most categories. Some properties (e.g. elevation, typography sub-properties) still as styles or hardcoded.
score_levels.4: All design values implemented as Figma Variables. No unexplained hardcoded values.

1.2_alias_chain_integrity.name: Alias Chain Integrity
1.2_alias_chain_integrity.description: Are semantic tokens correctly aliased to primitive tokens? Are alias chains intact, unbroken, and resolvable?
1.2_alias_chain_integrity.plain_description: Whether the three-layer token chain — from raw values through role-based names to component-specific names — is intact and every reference resolves correctly.

score_levels.0: No alias chains. All variables hold direct values.
score_levels.1: Alias chains exist for one category only. Most variables are flat.
score_levels.2: Alias chains for some categories (e.g. colour) but not others (spacing, typography).
score_levels.3: Alias chains for most categories with minor gaps or broken references.
score_levels.4: All semantic and component tokens alias to primitives. No broken chains. Fully resolvable.

1.3_token_architecture_depth.name: Token Architecture Depth
1.3_token_architecture_depth.description: Does the system implement all three layers: primitive, semantic, and component-level tokens? Are the layers correctly separated or collapsed?
1.3_token_architecture_depth.plain_description: Whether the system has all three required token layers — raw values, role-based names, and component-specific names — rather than collapsing or skipping layers.

score_levels.0: No layered architecture. Flat values only.
score_levels.1: Primitive layer exists. No semantic or component layers for most categories.
score_levels.2: Primitive and semantic layers for some categories. Component layer absent or partial.
score_levels.3: Three layers for most categories with minor gaps.
score_levels.4: Complete three-layer architecture (primitive, semantic, component) across all token categories.

1.4_primitive_naming.name: Primitive Naming
1.4_primitive_naming.description: Do primitive tokens follow a defined, machine-parseable naming convention using full words and slashes? Is the scale defined rather than exhaustive?
1.4_primitive_naming.plain_description: Whether the lowest-level token names follow a clear, predictable pattern that an AI tool can parse without guessing.

score_levels.0: No naming convention. Names are arbitrary or developer shorthand.
score_levels.1: Partial convention with frequent inconsistencies. Abbreviations common.
score_levels.2: Convention exists but not applied uniformly. Mix of full words and abbreviations.
score_levels.3: Consistent naming with full words and slashes. Minor exceptions.
score_levels.4: Machine-parseable naming throughout. Full words, slashes, intent-based names. Scale is defined.

1.5_token_format_machine_readability.name: Token Format and Machine-Readability
1.5_token_format_machine_readability.description: Are code-side tokens in a format an AI agent can parse without transformation (JSON, DTCG-compliant, CSS custom properties)?
1.5_token_format_machine_readability.plain_description: Whether the code-side token definitions are in a standard file format that an AI tool can read directly, without needing to run the application.

score_levels.0: No code-side token definitions. Tokens exist only in Figma.
score_levels.1: Tokens defined programmatically with no extraction. Agent must execute code to read values.
score_levels.2: Tokens extractable but require significant transformation.
score_levels.3: Tokens parseable via extraction script. Not natively in standard format.
score_levels.4: Tokens in standard machine-readable format (DTCG, Style Dictionary JSON, CSS custom properties).

1.6_token_documentation.name: Token Documentation
1.6_token_documentation.description: Are token descriptions or metadata present in both Figma and code? Can an agent determine when to use a token from its name and description alone?
1.6_token_documentation.plain_description: Whether individual tokens have written explanations of what they are for, so an AI tool can choose the right one without guessing.

score_levels.0: No descriptions on any tokens in Figma or code.
score_levels.1: Descriptions on less than 25% of tokens. Most categories undocumented.
score_levels.2: Descriptions on 25-60% of tokens. Some categories documented, others not.
score_levels.3: Descriptions on 60-90% of tokens. An agent can determine usage for most tokens.
score_levels.4: Descriptions on 90%+ of tokens. Agent can determine when to use any token from name and description alone.

2.1_component_to_token_binding.name: Component-to-Token Binding
2.1_component_to_token_binding.description: Are component properties (fill, stroke, spacing, typography) bound to tokens rather than hardcoded values?
2.1_component_to_token_binding.plain_description: Whether component colours, spacing, and type properties are wired to named tokens in the design file rather than set as raw numbers.

score_levels.0: No token bindings. All component properties use hardcoded values.
score_levels.1: Token bindings for one property type only (e.g. colour fills).
score_levels.2: Token bindings for colour and some typography. Spacing and other properties hardcoded.
score_levels.3: Most properties bound to tokens. Minor gaps in specific property types.
score_levels.4: All component properties bound to appropriate tokens. No unexplained hardcoded values.

2.2_component_api_composability.name: Component API Composability
2.2_component_api_composability.description: Are component APIs explicit, typed, and constrained to valid system values? Are components built to compose or are they monolithic?
2.2_component_api_composability.plain_description: Whether code components accept a defined set of valid options and are built to combine together, rather than accepting arbitrary values and behaving as self-contained units.

score_levels.0: No typed APIs. Components accept arbitrary props.
score_levels.1: Basic typing exists but APIs are permissive and monolithic.
score_levels.2: Some typed and constrained APIs. Mix of composable and monolithic patterns.
score_levels.3: Most APIs typed and constrained. Composition patterns present with minor gaps.
score_levels.4: All APIs explicit, typed, and constrained. Compound component and slot patterns throughout.

2.3_variant_completeness.name: Variant Completeness
2.3_variant_completeness.description: Are all meaningful visual and interactive states represented as named variants rather than style overrides?
2.3_variant_completeness.plain_description: Whether all meaningful states of a component — such as hover, focus, disabled, and error — exist as named, selectable variants rather than being applied manually each time.

score_levels.0: No named variants. States achieved through overrides only.
score_levels.1: Basic variants exist (e.g. size) but interactive states (hover, focus, disabled) absent.
score_levels.2: Some interactive states as variants. Inconsistent across component sets.
score_levels.3: Most states as named variants. Minor gaps on secondary components.
score_levels.4: All meaningful states (default, hover, focus, active, disabled, error, loading) as named variants.

2.4_escape_hatch_usage.name: Escape Hatch Usage
2.4_escape_hatch_usage.description: Are className, style, or equivalent overrides available? How frequently are they used in the consuming codebase?
2.4_escape_hatch_usage.plain_description: How often consuming code bypasses the component's intended API by applying custom styles directly — high frequency signals the component does not cover enough cases.

score_levels.0: Escape hatches are the primary styling mechanism. System has no constraint model.
score_levels.1: Escape hatches used in more than 50% of component instances.
score_levels.2: Escape hatches used in 25-50% of instances. API is insufficient for common cases.
score_levels.3: Escape hatches used in less than 25% of instances. Mostly for edge cases.
score_levels.4: Escape hatches rarely used (<5%). API covers all common use cases.

3.1_functional_intent_coverage.name: Functional Intent Coverage
3.1_functional_intent_coverage.description: Do components have descriptions that carry functional intent rather than visual description or implementation detail? Scores whether intent is present, not whether a description field exists.
3.1_functional_intent_coverage.plain_description: Whether component descriptions explain what each component is for, when to use it, and when not to — rather than just showing what it looks like or how to import it.

score_levels.0: No descriptions with functional intent. Descriptions absent or are code snippets / visual descriptions only.
score_levels.1: Less than 25% of components have intent-bearing descriptions.
score_levels.2: 25-60% of components have intent-bearing descriptions.
score_levels.3: 60-90% of components have intent-bearing descriptions.
score_levels.4: 90%+ of components have descriptions with clear functional intent.

3.2_documentation_indexing.name: Documentation Indexing
3.2_documentation_indexing.description: Is component documentation indexed via schemas, frontmatter, or queryable structure that lets an agent look things up rather than reading everything linearly?
3.2_documentation_indexing.plain_description: Whether documentation is organised so an AI tool can look up a specific answer directly, rather than having to read through large bodies of prose.

score_levels.0: No structured documentation. Prose-only or absent.
score_levels.1: Some documentation exists but is unstructured narrative.
score_levels.2: Partial structure (e.g. Storybook with inconsistent patterns).
score_levels.3: Mostly structured documentation with consistent schemas. Minor gaps.
score_levels.4: Fully structured, machine-queryable documentation. An agent can retrieve reliable answers.

3.3_intent_quality.name: Intent Quality
3.3_intent_quality.description: Is the documentation well-structured, appropriately concise, free of redundancy, and useful to an agent? Scored against the six-level documentation hierarchy: purpose, structure, intended behaviour, main use cases, error handling, edge cases.
3.3_intent_quality.plain_description: How well the documentation covers the full picture of a component or pattern — its purpose, anatomy, behaviour, use cases, error handling, and edge conditions — and whether it is written clearly enough to be directly useful to an AI tool.

score_levels.0: No structured intent documentation against the hierarchy. Zero levels covered.
score_levels.1: One or two hierarchy levels covered on some components. Quality is thin.
score_levels.2: Two to three levels covered on primary components. Gaps on secondary components and patterns.
score_levels.3: Four or more levels covered on primary components. Patterns documented. Minor quality gaps.
score_levels.4: Five or more levels covered consistently. Components and patterns both well-documented. Concise, non-redundant, agent-useful.

3.4_usage_guidance_structure.name: Usage Guidance Structure
3.4_usage_guidance_structure.description: Are usage constraints organised with labelled sections and explicit rules rather than narrative prose?
3.4_usage_guidance_structure.plain_description: Whether guidance about when and how to use components is written as clear, labelled rules rather than flowing prose that is harder to parse.

score_levels.0: No usage guidance documented.
score_levels.1: Qualitative guidance exists but is vague and unenforceable.
score_levels.2: Mix of rules and qualitative guidance. Do/don't patterns partially documented.
score_levels.3: Most constraints expressed as explicit rules. Do/don't patterns well-documented.
score_levels.4: All usage constraints are explicit, structured rules. Enforceable by tooling or agents.

3.5_in_file_documentation_structure.name: In-File Documentation Structure
3.5_in_file_documentation_structure.description: Does the Figma file contain structured documentation an agent can read directly, without external lookup? Scores whether documentation is co-located with components and readable from the file structure.
3.5_in_file_documentation_structure.plain_description: Whether the Figma file itself contains structured documentation that an AI tool can read, without having to look up a separate website or codebase.

score_levels.0: No documentation frames or extractable metadata in Figma.
score_levels.1: Documentation frames exist but contain no real content (placeholders only).
score_levels.2: Page-level metadata extractable. Sub-component descriptions mostly placeholders.
score_levels.3: Rich structural metadata extractable. Most documentation frames have real content.
score_levels.4: Complete documentation frames with real content at all levels. Variant inventories, anatomy, and composition patterns fully readable.

4.1_interaction_targets.name: Interaction Targets
4.1_interaction_targets.description: Minimum touch/click target sizes per platform. Mobile: 44x44pt (iOS), 48x48dp (Android). Web: 24x24px.
4.1_interaction_targets.plain_description: Whether clickable and tappable elements are large enough to meet the minimum size requirements for the platforms the system targets.

score_levels.0: No consideration of target sizes. Most interactive elements below minimum.
score_levels.1: Some elements meet minimums. Many below threshold.
score_levels.2: Most primary interactive elements meet minimums. Secondary elements inconsistent.
score_levels.3: Most interactive elements meet minimums. A small number of edge cases fall below.
score_levels.4: All interactive elements meet or exceed platform-appropriate minimums.

4.2_contrast_ratios.name: Contrast Ratios
4.2_contrast_ratios.description: WCAG AA minimum: 4.5:1 normal text, 3:1 large text and UI components. Derivable from token alias chain.
4.2_contrast_ratios.plain_description: Whether text and interface elements have enough colour contrast to meet the WCAG AA accessibility standard.

score_levels.0: No contrast consideration. Multiple failures against WCAG AA.
score_levels.1: Some primary colours meet AA. Many combinations fail.
score_levels.2: Most primary text/background pairs meet AA. Secondary combinations inconsistent.
score_levels.3: Primary and secondary palette tokens meet AA against intended backgrounds. Some lighter variants borderline.
score_levels.4: All text and UI component colour combinations meet WCAG AA. AAA met for primary content.

4.3_type_scale_consistency.name: Type Scale Consistency
4.3_type_scale_consistency.description: Is there a mathematical relationship between type scale steps (modular scale)?
4.3_type_scale_consistency.plain_description: Whether the type sizes in the system follow a mathematical progression rather than being chosen arbitrarily.

score_levels.0: No discernible scale relationship. Ad-hoc sizes throughout.
score_levels.1: Loose grouping of sizes with no consistent ratio.
score_levels.2: Partial scale with some consistent intervals. Multiple exceptions.
score_levels.3: Consistent ratio with one or two non-standard steps.
score_levels.4: Mathematical scale throughout (modular or derived). All steps follow the ratio.

4.4_type_completeness.name: Type Completeness
4.4_type_completeness.description: Does the type system define size, line height, letter spacing, weight, and case for every semantic role?
4.4_type_completeness.plain_description: Whether every text role — heading, body, label, caption, and so on — has all its properties defined: size, line height, letter spacing, weight, and case.

score_levels.0: No semantic type roles defined. Raw sizes only.
score_levels.1: Some roles defined with size only. Other properties missing.
score_levels.2: Core roles defined. Some roles lack full property binding (lineHeight, letterSpacing).
score_levels.3: Most roles fully defined. Minor gaps on secondary roles.
score_levels.4: All semantic roles (display, heading, body, caption, label, code) fully defined with all properties.

4.5_spacing_scale_regularity.name: Spacing Scale Regularity
4.5_spacing_scale_regularity.description: Is the spacing system built on a base unit (4pt, 8pt) with consistent progression?
4.5_spacing_scale_regularity.plain_description: Whether spacing values follow a consistent pattern based on a base unit, rather than being chosen individually for each component.

score_levels.0: No spacing system. Arbitrary values throughout.
score_levels.1: Some spacing values follow a pattern. Many arbitrary values.
score_levels.2: Base unit identifiable but progression inconsistent.
score_levels.3: Regular base unit with consistent progression. Minor exceptions.
score_levels.4: All spacing values are multiples of a common base unit. Progression is consistent.

4.6_grid_and_layout_system.name: Grid and Layout System
4.6_grid_and_layout_system.description: Is there a defined column grid with documented gutters, margins, and breakpoints?
4.6_grid_and_layout_system.plain_description: Whether the system defines a column grid with gutters, margins, and breakpoints that all layouts are expected to follow.

score_levels.0: No grid or layout system defined.
score_levels.1: Basic grid exists but gutters, margins, or breakpoints undefined.
score_levels.2: Grid with defined columns and breakpoints. Layout tokens not bound to a responsive system.
score_levels.3: Responsive grid with columns, gutters, margins, and breakpoints. Minor gaps.
score_levels.4: Complete responsive layout system with grid, breakpoints, safe areas, and platform chrome accounted for.

4.7_motion_duration_ranges.name: Motion Duration Ranges
4.7_motion_duration_ranges.description: UI transitions in 100-500ms range. Entry animations 200-300ms, exit animations 150-250ms.
4.7_motion_duration_ranges.plain_description: Whether animation timings are defined within the ranges considered appropriate for user interfaces (roughly 100–500 milliseconds).

score_levels.0: No motion duration tokens or definitions.
score_levels.1: Some durations defined but outside recommended ranges or inconsistent.
score_levels.2: Duration tokens exist. Some within range, some outside.
score_levels.3: Most durations within recommended ranges. Minor exceptions.
score_levels.4: All durations within ranges. Entry/exit asymmetry correctly applied.

4.8_motion_easing.name: Motion Easing
4.8_motion_easing.description: Standard easing defined (ease-out for entries, ease-in for exits). Linear easing flagged.
4.8_motion_easing.plain_description: Whether the system defines appropriate easing curves for animations — gentler on entry, sharper on exit — rather than defaulting to linear timing throughout.

score_levels.0: No easing curves defined.
score_levels.1: Some easing defined but linear used inappropriately or no entry/exit distinction.
score_levels.2: Standard easing curves exist. Inconsistent application.
score_levels.3: Easing curves defined and mostly correctly applied. Minor gaps.
score_levels.4: Standard easing for all transitions. Entry/exit curves distinct. Linear flagged or absent.

4.9_focus_state_presence.name: Focus State Presence
4.9_focus_state_presence.description: Every interactive component has a visible focus state. WCAG 2.4.7 requirement.
4.9_focus_state_presence.plain_description: Whether every interactive component has a visible focus indicator so keyboard users can see where they are on the page.

score_levels.0: No focus states on any component.
score_levels.1: Focus states on some primary interactive components only.
score_levels.2: Focus states on most primary components. Absent on secondary interactive elements.
score_levels.3: Focus states on most interactive components. Inconsistent on secondary elements.
score_levels.4: Every interactive component has a visible, distinct focus state.

4.10_error_state_coverage.name: Error State Coverage
4.10_error_state_coverage.description: Input components define error, success, and disabled states. Forms define error summary behaviour.
4.10_error_state_coverage.plain_description: Whether input components define what they look like in error, success, and disabled states.

score_levels.0: No error states on any component.
score_levels.1: Error states on one or two primary input components only.
score_levels.2: Error states on most primary inputs. Success and disabled states partial.
score_levels.3: Error states on form components with colour and helper text. Minor gaps on secondary inputs.
score_levels.4: Error, success, and disabled states on all input components. Error summary behaviour defined.

4.11_loading_state_coverage.name: Loading State Coverage
4.11_loading_state_coverage.description: Asynchronous actions have defined loading states. Skeleton screens, spinners, or progress indicators exist as system patterns.
4.11_loading_state_coverage.plain_description: Whether the system provides defined loading patterns — such as spinners or skeleton screens — for actions that take time to complete.

score_levels.0: No loading state patterns or components.
score_levels.1: A spinner or progress component exists but not integrated into other components.
score_levels.2: Loading components exist. Not integrated as variants on content-bearing components.
score_levels.3: Loading states integrated on most async components. Skeleton patterns present.
score_levels.4: Comprehensive loading patterns: spinners, progress bars, skeletons integrated across all async components.

4.12_empty_state_coverage.name: Empty State Coverage
4.12_empty_state_coverage.description: Lists, tables, and data-dependent views define empty states as system patterns.
4.12_empty_state_coverage.plain_description: Whether views that depend on data define what they show when that data is absent.

score_levels.0: No empty state patterns or components.
score_levels.1: One or two empty state examples exist. Not systematised.
score_levels.2: Empty states for some data-dependent views. Inconsistent.
score_levels.3: Empty states for most data-dependent views. Minor gaps.
score_levels.4: Systematic empty state patterns for all lists, tables, and data-dependent views.

4.13_colour_system_structure.name: Colour System Structure
4.13_colour_system_structure.description: Primitive palette has defined steps per hue. Semantic colours are role-based, not hue-based.
4.13_colour_system_structure.plain_description: Whether the colour system uses a defined palette with a clear progression of steps per hue, and assigns semantic roles such as 'error' or 'primary surface' rather than referencing raw hues by name.

score_levels.0: No colour system. Arbitrary hex values throughout.
score_levels.1: Some palette structure exists. Naming is hue-based (red-500) not role-based.
score_levels.2: Palette with defined steps. Mix of role-based and hue-based semantic naming.
score_levels.3: Structured palette with role-based semantics. Minor naming inconsistencies.
score_levels.4: Full colour system with defined steps per hue and role-based semantic naming throughout.

4.14_icon_sizing_consistency.name: Icon Sizing Consistency
4.14_icon_sizing_consistency.description: Icons use a defined size scale aligned with spacing system.
4.14_icon_sizing_consistency.plain_description: Whether icon sizes follow a defined scale that aligns with the spacing system, rather than being set individually per component.

score_levels.0: No icon sizing system. Arbitrary sizes.
score_levels.1: Some icons use consistent sizes. No defined scale.
score_levels.2: A size scale exists but not aligned with spacing system.
score_levels.3: Consistent size scale with default and variants. Not bound to variables.
score_levels.4: Defined icon size scale aligned with spacing system and bound to variables.

4.15_elevation_shadow_system.name: Elevation/Shadow System
4.15_elevation_shadow_system.description: Shadows or elevation follow a defined scale, not arbitrary per-component values.
4.15_elevation_shadow_system.plain_description: Whether depth and shadow effects follow a defined scale rather than being applied differently on each component.

score_levels.0: No elevation system. Arbitrary shadows per component.
score_levels.1: Some shadow styles exist. No consistent scale.
score_levels.2: Elevation scale partially defined. Mix of styles and arbitrary values.
score_levels.3: Defined elevation scale as styles. Not yet migrated to Variables.
score_levels.4: Complete elevation scale as Variables with consistent progression.

4.16_visibility_of_system_status.name: Visibility of System Status
4.16_visibility_of_system_status.description: Does the system define patterns for communicating state? Loading, progress, feedback, real-time validation.
4.16_visibility_of_system_status.plain_description: Whether the system provides defined patterns for communicating progress and feedback — such as loading indicators, validation messages, and success or error states — to users.

score_levels.0: Not addressed. No status communication patterns.
score_levels.1: Partially addressed. Some feedback components exist but no systematic approach.
score_levels.2: Systematically addressed. Multiple feedback patterns (alerts, progress, snackbar) defined.

4.17_match_between_system_and_real_world.name: Match Between System and Real World
4.17_match_between_system_and_real_world.description: Are component and variant names user-facing rather than developer-internal? Are content guidelines documented?
4.17_match_between_system_and_real_world.plain_description: Whether component names and content guidelines use language that matches how users and designers think, rather than developer shorthand.

score_levels.0: Not addressed. Names are developer-internal jargon.
score_levels.1: Partially addressed. Most names follow conventions but some are non-intuitive.
score_levels.2: Systematically addressed. Names are user-facing with content guidelines documented.

4.18_user_control_and_freedom.name: User Control and Freedom
4.18_user_control_and_freedom.description: Are undo, cancel, dismiss, and back navigation defined as system patterns?
4.18_user_control_and_freedom.plain_description: Whether the system provides defined patterns for cancelling, undoing, and dismissing actions.

score_levels.0: Not addressed. No dismissal or undo patterns.
score_levels.1: Partially addressed. Some dismissal patterns (Dialog, Drawer) but no undo or cancel patterns.
score_levels.2: Systematically addressed. Dismiss, cancel, undo, and destructive action confirmation patterns all defined.

4.19_consistency_and_standards.name: Consistency and Standards
4.19_consistency_and_standards.description: Are interaction patterns consistent across similar components? Are similar components structured consistently?
4.19_consistency_and_standards.plain_description: Whether similar components behave and are structured in the same way throughout the system.

score_levels.0: Not addressed. Significant inconsistency across similar components.
score_levels.1: Partially addressed. Some consistency but notable exceptions.
score_levels.2: Systematically addressed. Uniform variant naming and state conventions throughout.

4.20_error_prevention.name: Error Prevention
4.20_error_prevention.description: Do form components define validation rules? Are input constraints part of the component API?
4.20_error_prevention.plain_description: Whether the system provides patterns and constraints that help prevent users from making mistakes in the first place, rather than only handling errors after they occur.

score_levels.0: Not addressed. No validation or constraint patterns.
score_levels.1: Partially addressed. Form validation states exist but confirmation/safeguard patterns absent.
score_levels.2: Systematically addressed. Validation rules, constraints, and confirmation patterns defined.

4.21_recognition_rather_than_recall.name: Recognition Rather Than Recall
4.21_recognition_rather_than_recall.description: Are component options constrained to enumerated values? Documented with visual examples and decision trees?
4.21_recognition_rather_than_recall.plain_description: Whether components present users and developers with options to choose from, rather than requiring them to remember valid values or rules.

score_levels.0: Not addressed. No enumerated options or decision support.
score_levels.1: Partially addressed. Some constrained options. No decision trees.
score_levels.2: Systematically addressed. Recognition-based interaction patterns (autocomplete, select, menu) with enumerated options.

4.22_flexibility_and_efficiency.name: Flexibility and Efficiency
4.22_flexibility_and_efficiency.description: Are density variants available? Are shortcut and progressive disclosure patterns documented?
4.22_flexibility_and_efficiency.plain_description: Whether the system supports different information densities and provides patterns for progressive disclosure and shortcuts.

score_levels.0: Not addressed. No density or efficiency patterns.
score_levels.1: Partially addressed. Multiple sizes/density variants exist but keyboard shortcuts and progressive disclosure absent.
score_levels.2: Systematically addressed. Density variants, shortcuts, and progressive disclosure all documented.

4.23_aesthetic_and_minimalist_design.name: Aesthetic and Minimalist Design
4.23_aesthetic_and_minimalist_design.description: Is there a defined visual hierarchy system? Are information density and truncation rules documented?
4.23_aesthetic_and_minimalist_design.plain_description: Whether the system defines rules for visual hierarchy and information density, so interfaces show only what is needed and present it in a clear order.

score_levels.0: Not addressed. No visual hierarchy or density guidelines.
score_levels.1: Partially addressed. Design principles followed but density and truncation rules undocumented.
score_levels.2: Systematically addressed. Visual hierarchy system, density guidelines, and truncation rules all documented.

4.24_error_recovery.name: Error Recovery
4.24_error_recovery.description: Are error messages structured (what happened, why, what to do)? Are recovery actions defined as patterns?
4.24_error_recovery.plain_description: Whether error messages in the system are structured to explain what went wrong and offer a clear next step for recovery.

score_levels.0: Not addressed. No error recovery patterns.
score_levels.1: Partially addressed. Some error messages exist but recovery actions not formalised.
score_levels.2: Systematically addressed. Structured error messages with retry, undo, and rollback patterns.

4.25_help_and_documentation.name: Help and Documentation
4.25_help_and_documentation.description: Do components have descriptions explaining when to use them? Is there a searchable pattern library? Are X vs Y decisions documented?
4.25_help_and_documentation.plain_description: Whether the system provides guidance that helps people decide which component to use and how to use it correctly.

score_levels.0: Not addressed. No help patterns or contextual documentation.
score_levels.1: Partially addressed. Some component descriptions exist. No decision guides.
score_levels.2: Systematically addressed. Tooltip help, onboarding patterns, searchable library, and X vs Y decision guides.

4.26_visual_hierarchy.name: Visual Hierarchy
4.26_visual_hierarchy.description: Are elevation/layering rules documented? Are grouping patterns defined with spacing rules?
4.26_visual_hierarchy.plain_description: Whether the system defines rules for how elements should be layered and grouped to guide attention and communicate relative importance.

score_levels.0: Not addressed. No hierarchy guidelines.
score_levels.1: Partially addressed. Typography scale and elevation support hierarchy but no explicit guidelines.
score_levels.2: Systematically addressed. Hierarchy guidelines, emphasis levels, layering rules, and grouping patterns all documented.

4.27_visual_rhythm_and_proportion.name: Visual Rhythm and Proportion
4.27_visual_rhythm_and_proportion.description: Is vertical spacing governed by a system? Are component internal proportions consistent?
4.27_visual_rhythm_and_proportion.plain_description: Whether the system defines a consistent spatial model that governs vertical spacing and component proportions, so layouts feel visually coherent.

score_levels.0: Not addressed. No spatial model.
score_levels.1: Partially addressed. Base unit provides rhythm but proportional relationships not formalised.
score_levels.2: Systematically addressed. Documented spatial model with consistent proportions and rhythm.

5.1_naming_convention_consistency.name: Naming Convention Consistency
5.1_naming_convention_consistency.description: Are naming conventions consistent across tokens, components, styles, and files? Do names use full words and slashes?
5.1_naming_convention_consistency.plain_description: Whether tokens, components, styles, and files all follow the same naming patterns, making the system predictable to navigate and extend.

score_levels.0: No naming conventions. Inconsistent throughout.
score_levels.1: Conventions exist for one category. Others inconsistent.
score_levels.2: Conventions exist but not applied uniformly. Mix of patterns.
score_levels.3: Consistent conventions across most categories. Minor inconsistencies.
score_levels.4: Uniform naming conventions across tokens, components, styles, and files.

5.2_versioning_and_changelog_discipline.name: Versioning and Changelog Discipline
5.2_versioning_and_changelog_discipline.description: Is there a structured changelog? Semantic versioning in use?
5.2_versioning_and_changelog_discipline.plain_description: Whether the system records its changes in a structured log and uses version numbers that communicate what kind of change was made.

score_levels.0: No versioning or changelog.
score_levels.1: Version numbers exist but no structured changelog.
score_levels.2: Version metadata present. No structured changelog.
score_levels.3: Semantic versioning with structured changelog. Minor gaps.
score_levels.4: Full semantic versioning with structured, complete changelog. Reliable upgrade path reasoning.

5.3_contribution_standards.name: Contribution Standards
5.3_contribution_standards.description: Are there explicit, machine-readable contribution guidelines?
5.3_contribution_standards.plain_description: Whether there are clear, documented rules for how changes to the system should be proposed and reviewed.

score_levels.0: No contribution guidelines.
score_levels.1: Basic guidelines exist but vague or incomplete.
score_levels.2: Contribution guidelines present. Not machine-readable.
score_levels.3: Structured contribution guidelines with templates and checklists.
score_levels.4: Machine-readable contribution guidelines. An agent can propose valid changes.

5.4_deprecation_patterns.name: Deprecation Patterns
5.4_deprecation_patterns.description: Are deprecated components and tokens clearly marked with migration paths?
5.4_deprecation_patterns.plain_description: Whether removed or replaced components and tokens are clearly labelled as deprecated and accompanied by instructions for migrating to their replacements.

score_levels.0: No deprecation markers anywhere.
score_levels.1: Some deprecation markers but no migration paths documented.
score_levels.2: Deprecation markers on most deprecated items. Migration paths partial.
score_levels.3: Deprecation markers with migration paths on most items.
score_levels.4: All deprecated items clearly marked with complete migration paths and sunset dates.

5.5_test_coverage.name: Test Coverage
5.5_test_coverage.description: Are components covered by visual regression tests, unit tests, or accessibility tests?
5.5_test_coverage.plain_description: Whether components are covered by automated tests for visual appearance, functional behaviour, and accessibility.

score_levels.0: No tests.
score_levels.1: Minimal test coverage. One test type on some components.
score_levels.2: Partial coverage across test types. Gaps on secondary components.
score_levels.3: Good coverage with visual regression, unit, and accessibility tests. Minor gaps.
score_levels.4: Comprehensive test coverage across all types and all components.

5.6_adoption_visibility.name: Adoption Visibility
5.6_adoption_visibility.description: Is there tooling to understand which consuming applications use which components at which versions?
5.6_adoption_visibility.plain_description: Whether the system has tooling to show which products use which components at which version.

score_levels.0: No adoption tracking.
score_levels.1: Basic dependency tracking (package.json) but no component-level visibility.
score_levels.2: Some component-level tracking. Incomplete.
score_levels.3: Component-level adoption tracking across most consuming applications.
score_levels.4: Full adoption visibility: component usage, version distribution, and upgrade status across all consumers.

5.7_code_consistency_and_pattern_predict.name: Code Consistency and Pattern Predictability
5.7_code_consistency_and_pattern_predict.description: Do components follow consistent internal structure? Are conventions enforced by linting?
5.7_code_consistency_and_pattern_predict.plain_description: Whether components are built according to a consistent internal structure that an AI tool can learn and rely on when generating or modifying code.

score_levels.0: No consistent patterns. High structural divergence.
score_levels.1: Some patterns exist but not enforced. Significant divergence.
score_levels.2: Conventions documented but partially enforced. Moderate divergence.
score_levels.3: Conventions enforced by linting. Minor divergence.
score_levels.4: Uniform internal structure enforced by tooling. Predictable patterns throughout.

6.1_token_value_parity.name: Token Value Parity
6.1_token_value_parity.description: Do the token values in code match those in the Figma Variables? Drift means an agent working from one source will generate incorrect code.
6.1_token_value_parity.plain_description: Whether the actual values assigned to tokens in code match those in Figma, so an AI tool working from either source produces the same result.

score_levels.0: No matched tokens or widespread value divergence.
score_levels.1: Less than 75% of matched tokens have identical values.
score_levels.2: 75-90% value match. Multiple categories with drift.
score_levels.3: 90-98% value match. Drift limited to a few specific tokens.
score_levels.4: 98%+ value match. Any remaining differences are documented as intentional.

6.2_token_naming_parity.name: Token Naming Parity
6.2_token_naming_parity.description: Do the token names in code match the Figma Variable names? Misalignment forces human translation.
6.2_token_naming_parity.plain_description: Whether tokens are named consistently between Figma and code, so there is no manual translation step when moving between the two.

score_levels.0: No naming alignment. Completely different naming schemes.
score_levels.1: Some shared naming roots but different conventions.
score_levels.2: Naming aligned for some categories. Others diverge.
score_levels.3: Naming aligned across most categories. Syntactic differences predictable.
score_levels.4: 100% naming alignment. Conventions match or differences are systematic and documented.

6.3_component_naming_parity.name: Component Naming Parity
6.3_component_naming_parity.description: Do component names, variant names, and prop names align between Figma and code?
6.3_component_naming_parity.plain_description: Whether component names, variant names, and property names match between Figma and code.

score_levels.0: No component naming alignment.
score_levels.1: Some base component names match. Variant and prop names diverge.
score_levels.2: Base names match for most components. Variant naming conventions differ.
score_levels.3: Base and variant names align. Syntactic differences predictable (e.g. Size=Medium vs size='medium').
score_levels.4: Full naming alignment. Component, variant, and prop names match or follow documented mapping rules.

6.4_variant_and_state_coverage_parity.name: Variant and State Coverage Parity
6.4_variant_and_state_coverage_parity.description: Are all Figma-defined states implemented in code? Are all coded states specified in Figma?
6.4_variant_and_state_coverage_parity.plain_description: Whether every visual state defined in Figma has a corresponding implementation in code, and vice versa.

score_levels.0: No cross-referencing. Major gaps in both directions.
score_levels.1: Some states match. Many Figma-only or code-only states.
score_levels.2: Most primary states match. Secondary states inconsistent.
score_levels.3: Comprehensive state matching. Not deeply cross-referenced at scale.
score_levels.4: All states verified as matching between Figma and code. Gaps documented.

6.5_behaviour_parity.name: Behaviour Parity
6.5_behaviour_parity.description: Are there interactive behaviours in code with no corresponding Figma specification, or vice versa?
6.5_behaviour_parity.plain_description: Whether interactive behaviours — such as hover, focus, and keyboard navigation — are specified in both Figma and code rather than being decided unilaterally in one place.

score_levels.0: No behaviour comparison. Unilateral design decisions in both directions.
score_levels.1: Some behaviours match. Major gaps in hover, focus, keyboard navigation.
score_levels.2: Most primary behaviours specified in both. Gaps in secondary interactions.
score_levels.3: Comprehensive behaviour parity. Minor gaps documented.
score_levels.4: Full behaviour parity. All interactive behaviours specified in Figma and implemented in code.

6.6_documentation_of_parity_gaps.name: Documentation of Parity Gaps
6.6_documentation_of_parity_gaps.description: Are known gaps between Figma and code tracked and documented? Undocumented gaps become silent agent errors.
6.6_documentation_of_parity_gaps.plain_description: Whether known differences between the Figma design and the code are tracked and documented, so AI tools know which source to follow.

score_levels.0: No documented parity gaps. Gaps exist but are undocumented.
score_levels.1: Some gaps acknowledged informally. No structured register.
score_levels.2: Parity gap register exists with partial coverage. Intentional vs drift not distinguished.
score_levels.3: Gap register with most gaps documented. Intentional gaps marked.
score_levels.4: Complete parity gap register. All gaps tracked, classified as intentional or drift, and reviewed regularly.
